title: Infinite Powers
subtitle: How Calculus Reveals the Secrets of the Universe

authors.name: Steven Strogatz
knownFor: Author of Sync and The Joy of x
Contributor to The New York Times Opinionator blog

description: Steven Strogatz's sweeping history of calculus tells the story of how one of humanity's greatest intellectual inventions — a mathematical framework invented to understand change and motion — was built by real people over more than two millennia, and why it is the language that underlies everything from planetary orbits to smartphone GPS. Beginning with the ancient Greece of Archimedes and the method of exhaustion, moving through the analytic geometry revolution of Descartes and Fermat, and arriving at the crowning achievement of Newton and Leibniz in the 17th century, Strogatz shows how each of these thinkers added a single crucial idea to a collective enterprise. The book's organizing metaphor is the three pillars of calculus: slicing (breaking a complex shape into simpler pieces), gluing (reassembling those pieces into something new), and changing (using the derivative to understand how quantities vary). These three operations, Strogatz argues, are not just mathematical techniques — they are a habit of thought that has shaped science, engineering, and our model of the universe. The closing chapters apply calculus to the modern world: differential equations that model epidemics, fluid dynamics that describe blood flow, and the GPS algorithm that relies on general relativity's corrections to satellite clocks.
keyIdeas: The method of exhaustion, invented by Archimedes 2,000 years before calculus, was the true ancestor of integral calculus — Eudoxus and Archimedes calculated pi and areas by adding up infinitely many slices
Descartes and Fermat independently invented analytic geometry, making it possible to describe curves with equations and turning geometry into algebra
Fermat's method of tangents, developed in the 1620s for calculating maximums and minimums, was the direct ancestor of differential calculus

The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus — connecting the slice-and-glue process (integration) to the changing process (differentiation) — was discovered independently by Newton and Leibniz in the 1660s-1670s and is arguably the single most important theorem in all of mathematics
Calculus enabled the Scientific Revolution: Newton used it to derive the laws of motion and universal gravitation; without calculus, the modern age of physics and engineering cannot be bootstrapped
Differential equations — equations involving rates of change — are calculus's most powerful application: they model epidemic spread, population dynamics, fluid flow, and planetary orbits

The infinity concept that calculus tamed — infinitely many slices, infinitely small steps — was philosophically controversial for centuries and required new logical foundations from Cauchy and Weierstrass in the 19th century

Modern GPS systems must apply corrections from Einstein's general theory of relativity (gravitational time dilation) to satellite clocks; without calculus-derived corrections, positioning errors would accumulate at 10 km per day
Strogatz frames calculus as a human story: its creation involved bitter priority disputes, genuine collaboration across centuries, and the gradual accumulation of insight by many hands
keyTakeaways: Calculus is not just a set of techniques — it is a perspective on the world that recognizes everything as continuous, changeable, and composed of infinitesimal parts
The three operations of calculus — slicing, gluing, and changing — correspond to the fundamental processes of scientific thinking: decomposition, recomposition, and analysis of dynamics

The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus is the bridge: it shows that the infinite sum of infinitesimal changes (integration) and the instantaneous rate of change (differentication) are two sides of the same coin
Archimedes was doing integral calculus 1,800 years before Newton; the history of calculus is a story of rediscovery and building on ideas that were ahead of their time
Newton and Leibniz's bitter priority dispute over who invented calculus harmed both men and slowed mathematical progress; the real story is one of collective, cross-generational discovery
Differential equations are the language of the universe: from how a virus spreads to how a galaxy rotates to how blood moves through a capillary, the math is the same
Calculus made the modern world possible: the Industrial Revolution was a calculus revolution; every machine, every bridge, every circuit depends on it
The concept of infinity that calculus requires was only put on rigorous foundations in the 19th century by Cauchy and Weierstrass, who replaced intuitive infinitesimals with formal limits
You do not need to be a mathematician to appreciate this story: calculus is the shared intellectual heritage of every person who lives in a world shaped by science and technology
whoShouldRead: Anyone curious about where modern mathematics and science came from
Students beginning calculus who want to understand the 'why' before tackling the 'how'
Science and engineering professionals who want to reconnect with the intellectual history underpinning their work
Readers of popular science who enjoyed biographies of mathematical ideas
People who think they are 'not math people' — Strogatz writes for general audiences with genuine warmth and clarity
Educators teaching calculus or physics who want narrative material to enliven their subject
whoShouldSkip: Readers seeking a rigorous calculus textbook or problem-solving workbook
Mathematicians looking for original proofs or technical depth on advanced analysis
Those already deeply familiar with the history of mathematics who want primary-source treatment
Readers who prefer anecdotal memoirs over broad intellectual history
